Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.

response_raw:
{
    "mode": "human",
    "topic": "How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.",
    "opinion": "As a plumber from Bari who's been working with my hands for decades, I approach discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines the same way I approach a tricky pipe installation - with practical experience and a focus on what actually works. At 57, I've seen enough to know that real-world results matter more than fancy theories. When I'm on social media for hours each day, which I admit I do too much of lately with my depression, I see all kinds of arguments about vaccines. My Buddhist beliefs teach me to approach these conversations with compassion, but my low openness score means I'm not easily swayed by new ideas that don't match what I've observed. What I've observed is simple: vaccines work. I've seen it in my own family and community here in Bari. When talking about this with customers while fixing their pipes or with friends during calcio matches, I keep it straightforward. I don't use complicated medical terms - I talk about how hospitals were overflowing before vaccines and how they're manageable now. My moderate agreeableness means I'll listen to other views, but my high extraversion means I'm not shy about sharing what I've seen with my own eyes. As someone with a vocational diploma, not a university degree, I respect expertise but also trust practical experience. My father had no formal education and my mother had a vocational diploma like me, so we come from people who value hands-on knowledge. When people bring up conspiracy theories, I point to the plumbers, electricians, and construction workers who got vaccinated and kept working - people like us who keep society running. My low neuroticism helps me stay calm in these discussions, even when they get heated. I've found that sharing my own story - getting vaccinated so I could keep working to support my wife and child - resonates more than statistics. In the end, it comes down to this: we take practical steps to protect what matters, whether that's sealing a pipe properly or getting a shot to protect our community. Both require trusting the experts who've trained for years to do their jobs right.",

Focused on observable results and community protection as key arguments.",

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.
response_parsed.opinion: As a plumber from Bari who's been working with my hands for decades, I approach discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines the same way I approach a tricky pipe installation - with practical experience and a focus on what actually works. At 57, I've seen enough to know that real-world results matter more than fancy theories. When I'm on social media for hours each day, which I admit I do too much of lately with my depression, I see all kinds of arguments about vaccines. My Buddhist beliefs teach me to approach these conversations with compassion, but my low openness score means I'm not easily swayed by new ideas that don't match what I've observed. What I've observed is simple: vaccines work. I've seen it in my own family and community here in Bari. When talking about this with customers while fixing their pipes or with friends during calcio matches, I keep it straightforward. I don't use complicated medical terms - I talk about how hospitals were overflowing before vaccines and how they're manageable now. My moderate agreeableness means I'll listen to other views, but my high extraversion means I'm not shy about sharing what I've seen with my own eyes. As someone with a vocational diploma, not a university degree, I respect expertise but also trust practical experience. My father had no formal education and my mother had a vocational diploma like me, so we come from people who value hands-on knowledge. When people bring up conspiracy theories, I point to the plumbers, electricians, and construction workers who got vaccinated and kept working - people like us who keep society running. My low neuroticism helps me stay calm in these discussions, even when they get heated. I've found that sharing my own story - getting vaccinated so I could keep working to support my wife and child - resonates more than statistics. In the end, it comes down to this: we take practical steps to protect what matters, whether that's sealing a pipe properly or getting a shot to protect our community. Both require trusting the experts who've trained for years to do their jobs right.
